reasoning:
The question asks for the probability that the daily average temperature at Juan de Nova Island (Southern Hemisphere, relatively tropical, ~17°S) will be higher on a set of future resolution dates than it was on 2025-10-26. Since specific resolution dates are omitted from the text but the question closes in late January 2026, we can deduce these dates are likely in November, December, and January. These months constitute the late spring and summer in the Southern Hemisphere, where average daily temperatures incrementally climb from ~27.5 °C in late October to around 28.5–29.5 °C in January. 
However, variance does play a role. If temperatures on 2025-10-26 happened to be unusually high (as the reading of 28.788 °C on 10-16 might suggest, leaning toward a warm month), it would set a strict baseline. The condition expects the temperature to be higher on *each* of the subsequent dates. We model this as comparing a single draw $X$ (Oct 26 temp) near to the minimum of $n$ draws $Y_i$ (temperatures of summer months). With an ascending mean trend but non-zero daily variations (standard deviation around ~0.8 °C for tropical oceans), the likelihood that all subsequent sample dates register warmer than the October reading is approximately 60-65% (integration of normal overlap yields ~0.62 for 3 future dates).
